_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the technical fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.cape: The Shiller CAPE (Cyclically Adjusted Price-to-Earnings ratio) measures the price of the S&P 500 divided by the average real earnings over the past 10 years, smoothing out business cycle fluctuations. As of July 2026, the CAPE stands at 42.0, well above its 140-year average of 17.3 and only slightly below the all-time peak of 44.19 in December 1999. Historically, a CAPE above 39 has signaled low forward returns, with the S&P 500 averaging about -4% over the next year and -20% over two years, reflecting the risk of valuation mean reversion. However, high CAPE readings do not provide precise timing signals for market declines and can persist during prolonged bull markets, often driven by structural or liquidity factors.
notes.buffett: The Buffett Indicator, defined as the ratio of total US market capitalization to GDP, is a broad valuation gauge assessing market size relative to economic output. At 231.7% in June 2026, this reading far exceeds prior peaks: the dot-com bubble high of 146% and the 2007 pre-crisis peak of 110%. Warren Buffett has famously termed levels above 200% as 'playing with fire,' highlighting elevated systemic risk from stretched valuations. Economically, this ratio captures how market valuations outpace fundamentals, implying potential corrections if earning expectations fall. Nonetheless, shifts in market structure or GDP measurement nuances can affect interpretation, limiting its precision as a tactical timing tool.
notes.concentration: Concentration metrics assess the market share held by top firms within an index, revealing risks associated with sector or name dominance. Currently, the top 5 S&P 500 stocks constitute about 29.8%, while the tech sector alone represents 48% of the index in 2026. Notably, the top 10 stocks account for 41%, up from 17.5% in 2015, indicating an increasing concentration. High concentration can exacerbate volatility and correlated drawdowns, making broad market indices more sensitive to the fortunes of a few large companies. However, concentration may reflect genuine economic leadership or innovation clusters, which complicates risk assessments based solely on this metric.
notes.yieldcurve: The yield curve spread between the 10-year Treasury and 3-month Treasury notes, a classic recession indicator, currently stands at 0.74 percentage points as of July 2026. This positive spread suggests a normal, upward-sloping yield curve, which historically implies ongoing economic expansion. The NY Fed’s model assigns a 31% probability to US recession within the next year despite this, reflecting other risks. Yield curve inversions historically precede recessions by several months to years, signaling tightening credit conditions that can shrink liquidity and depress equities. Nonetheless, its predictive power fluctuates with monetary policy regimes and can produce false positives, so it should be considered alongside other indicators.
notes.drypowder: Dry powder refers to cash reserves or liquid capital that investors have available for deployment. Berkshire Hathaway’s cash holdings reached a record $397.4 billion in Q1 2026, indicating significant firepower for opportunistic buying. However, 79% of institutions expect their dry powder to decline, implying increased deployment or constrained liquidity ahead. High dry powder can support market stability by providing a buyer base when valuations fall, but rapid drawdowns may exacerbate selling pressure. While notable, dry powder alone does not dictate market turns; its impact depends on timing, sentiment, and broader liquidity conditions.
notes.finale: Valuation metrics like CAPE and the Buffett Indicator set the potential magnitude of a market correction, anchoring expectations to historically extreme levels. However, these metrics do not pinpoint timing—the 'music stopping'—which is driven by liquidity shocks disrupting market participation. A pertinent trigger window is December 2026 to May 2027, when IPO lock-up expirations for high-profile companies like SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic could introduce substantial selling pressure as insiders become free to sell. This influx of supply can strain liquidity, potentially ending the current bull market momentum by removing the underpinning of cheap money and momentum investors. Thus, valuation extremes raise the risk, but liquidity events like lock-up expirations serve as the likely catalyst.
